questions.question: Explain the difference between internal fragmentation and external fragmentation. Which memory management techniques cause each type?
keyPoints: Internal fragmentation: wasted space within allocated memory blocks due to fixed allocation units
External fragmentation: scattered free memory blocks preventing large allocations
Paging causes internal fragmentation (unused part of last frame)
Contiguous allocation causes external fragmentation (scattered holes)

questions.question: Describe the working set model for memory management. How does it help in preventing thrashing?
keyPoints: Working set is the set of pages actively used by a process in a given time interval
OS maintains enough frames for each process's working set
If working set size > allocated frames, thrashing occurs
Keeping working set in memory minimizes page faults and prevents thrashing

questions.question: What is copy-on-write? Explain its role in process creation and memory optimization.
keyPoints: Parent and child processes initially share the same memory pages
Pages are marked as read-only initially
Copy is made only when a process writes to a shared page
Saves memory and speeds up process creation

questions.question: Explain demand paging. Why is it considered efficient compared to loading all pages at process startup?
keyPoints: Pages loaded only when referenced (on-demand)
Reduces initial load time and memory usage
Not all pages of a program are needed during execution
Pages for unused code/functions never loaded into memory

questions.question: What is Belady's anomaly? Under what conditions does it occur?
keyPoints: Belady's anomaly: increasing frames can increase page faults
Observed in FIFO algorithm
Occurs because FIFO doesn't consider page usage patterns
LRU and Optimal do not suffer from this anomaly

questions.question: Explain the difference between paging and segmentation. How can they be combined?
keyPoints: Paging: fixed-size blocks, physical view, eliminates external fragmentation
Segmentation: variable-size logical units, programmer's view
Segmentation with paging: divide segments into pages
Combines benefits: logical organization + efficient memory use

questions.question: Describe the steps involved in handling a page fault by the operating system.
keyPoints: Trap to OS, identifying the virtual address causing fault
Check if address is valid and legal
Locate the required page on secondary storage
Find a free frame in physical memory
Load the page from disk into the allocated frame
Update page table with new frame number
Restart the interrupted instruction

questions.question: Discuss the various page replacement algorithms. Compare FIFO, LRU, and Optimal algorithms with respect to performance and implementation complexity.
keyPoints: FIFO: Simple implementation using queue, suffers from Belady's anomaly, moderate page faults
LRU: Better performance than FIFO, difficult to implement (needs hardware support or stack), no anomaly
Optimal: Minimum page faults theoretically possible, impossible to implement practically (needs future knowledge)
Trade-off: Optimal > LRU > FIFO in performance; FIFO > LRU > Optimal in implementation ease
Clock algorithm: Practical approximation of LRU using reference bits

questions.question: Explain the concept of thrashing. Discuss its causes, symptoms, and the techniques used by operating systems to prevent thrashing.
keyPoints: Thrashing: excessive paging activity consuming CPU time
Causes: too many processes competing for too few frames, working set exceeds allocated frames
Symptoms: high CPU utilization but low throughput, constant page faults, system seems busy but no work gets done
Prevention: working set model, page fault frequency control, admission control, local replacement
OS monitors page fault rate and adjusts degree of multiprogramming accordingly

questions.question: Explain the structure of a page table and various techniques for organizing page tables to reduce memory overhead.
keyPoints: Page table contains: frame number, valid/invalid bit, protection bits, reference bit, modify bit
Simple page table: one entry per virtual page - inefficient for large address spaces
Hierarchical (multi-level) page tables: tree structure reducing memory needs
Inverted page tables: one entry per physical frame instead of virtual page
Hashed page tables: using hash for faster lookups
Page table entries also contain: access control, dirty bit for write-back

questions.question: Explain virtual memory management. How does it create the illusion of a larger memory than physically available? Discuss its advantages.
keyPoints: Virtual memory combines RAM and disk to provide larger address space
Uses paging/segmentation to map virtual addresses to physical
Pages not in memory trigger page faults, loaded from disk on demand
Illusion created: each process believes it has exclusive access to entire memory
Advantages: more processes, process protection, memory sharing, easier programming, relocation
